chapter_summary: In this 1980 address, Hirsch argues that literacy instruction has mistakenly focused almost exclusively on the 'craft' of writing (syntax, organization, mechanics) while neglecting the 'cultural dimension.' He contends that writing is like an iceberg, where the visible mechanics represent only a small fraction of the communication process, which relies heavily on a vast, invisible base of tacit cultural knowledge shared between writer and reader.

claims.text: Mina Shaughnessy exemplified literate culture at its best through a combination of social purpose, integrity, eloquence, and a sense of style.
claims.source_passage: as Mina lives in my memory of her, she is an Arnoldian figure-a poet, essayist, critic, even an inspector of schools, and at the same time a person who integrated all these roles not just in service of a powerful cultural mission, but also in exemplification of literate culture at its best: social purpose, integrity, eloquence, and something very Arnoldian-a sense of style.

claims.text: The most important dimensions of writing and teaching writing are often neglected because they are so obvious and elementary that they are taken for granted.
claims.source_passage: The act of writing and the teaching of writing are so complex and elusive that we sometimes neglect their most important dimensions just because those aspects are so obvious and elementary that we take them for granted.

claims.text: The back-to-basics movement was fostered by a shocked recognition that basic dimensions of writing, such as spelling and motor skills, had been neglected.
claims.source_passage: Shocked recognition of this has fostered, for instance, the back to-basics movement with its renewed emphasis on spelling, motor skills, traditional practice in usage, and so on.

claims.text: The cultural aspect of writing is a fundamental and obvious dimension that has suffered from educational neglect.
claims.source_passage: The main subject of my talk today concerns another basic aspect of writing, one so fundamental and obvious, that it too has suffered neglect. I mean the cultural aspect of writing.

claims.text: The majority of literature regarding composition focuses exclusively on the craft of writing, such as coherence, organization, and syntax.
claims.source_passage: That is why virtually all that is written about composition devotes itself to the craft of writing-to coherence, to pre-writing, organization, syntax, sentence variety, and the like.

claims.text: The craft of writing is only half the story of literacy.
claims.source_passage: But in the past few months, and in the light of our recent large-scale experiments at Virginia, sponsored by the National Endowment for the Humanities, I have begun to realize that the craft of writing is only half the story.

claims.text: Efforts to improve writing instruction will only be partly successful as long as they are narrowly oriented toward writing as a craft.
claims.source_passage: But I should like to suggest in this talk why this laudable effort can only be partly successful so long as it is narrowly oriented to writing as a craft which can be even more efficiently taught, as research uncovers ever more efficient ways of teaching it.

claims.text: The craft-approach to writing neglects the huge domain of tacit knowledge that is never written down but remains operative.
claims.source_passage: I said that the craft-approach neglects the cultural dimension of writing. Alternatively, one could say that we have stressed the process and product of writing at the expense of the huge domain of tacit knowledge which is never written down at all, but which, though quite invisible, is just as operative as the visible written word.

claims.text: A writing task is comparable to an iceberg, where visible mechanics are the tip and tacit cultural knowledge is the much larger invisible base.
claims.source_passage: A writing task could be compared to an iceberg whose visible tip is arrangement, syntax, rhetoric, spelling, coherence and so on, but whose much bigger invisible base is tacit cultural knowledge

claims.text: Tacit cultural knowledge includes knowledge of the topic and knowledge of what others also know and expect about the topic, form, writer, and world.
claims.source_passage: tacit cultural knowledge- not just linguistic knowledge, and knowledge about the topic, but also, and most important, knowledge of what others also know and expect about the topic, about the form, about the writer, and about the world.

claims.text: Human perception and memory of sentences focus on what the linguistic traces invisibly entail rather than the traces themselves.
claims.source_passage: The tests concerned whether we perceive and remember what a sentence says as a linguistic trace, or whether we perceive and remember, instead, what the linguistic traces invisibly entail.

claims.text: The cultural dimension of literacy consists of a whole system of unspoken, tacit knowledge shared between writer and reader.
claims.source_passage: In short, the cultural dimension is that whole system of unspoken, tacit knowledge that is shared between writer and reader.

claims.text: Readers understand and remember an invisible, culturally shared component often referred to as 'knowledge of the world.'
claims.source_passage: Readers also understand and remember an invisible, culturally shared component which many linguistic model builders now put into a box labeled 'knowledge of the world.'

claims.text: Writing requires a sense of the other person's range of knowledge and expectations.
claims.source_passage: What they lacked was a sense of the other person's range of knowledge and expectations. They lacked, that is, a good sense of what the other person knew.

claims.text: American fifteen-year-olds are deficient in tasks that approximate the writing task because they lack a sense of the reader's knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The American fifteen-year-old subjects chosen by Kraus and Glucksberg were the products of eight or nine years in our school systems. They were *not* trapped in egocentricity, but they *were* deficient in a task which closely approximates the writing task.

claims.text: The deficiency in student writing performance is not caused by a lack of vocabulary, grammar, or syntax.
claims.source_passage: And what they clearly lacked was not vocabulary, or grammar, or syntax. What they lacked was a sense of the other person's range of knowledge and expectations.

claims.text: There is no developmental reason why a fifteen-year-old should be culturally illiterate.
claims.source_passage: I believe that there is no developmental reason why a 15-year-old should be culturally illiterate to this degree.

claims.text: Good education is the specific antidote to cultural illiteracy.
claims.source_passage: My point is, of course, that good education is the specific antidote to cultural illiteracy, and that improvements in literate education would affect performance in this kind of experimental task, which is so analogous to a writing task.

claims.text: Literate adults perform perfectly on communication tasks because they use prolix descriptions that relate unfamiliar items to shared, familiar shapes.
claims.source_passage: adults always managed to score 100 per cent on their first try. And one noteworthy feature of this adult performance was that... adults tended to be very prolix in their descriptions, as though they realized that the shapes would be unfamiliar to the other person, and would therefore have to be carefully related to other shared and familiar shapes.

claims.text: The experiment results for 15-year-olds would be reversed if the subjects were highly literate fifteen-year-olds who were practiced readers and writers.
claims.source_passage: No tests were run, apparently, with pairs of highly literate fifteen year-olds, who were at once practiced readers and proficient writers... And I will wager that the results in such cases would be precisely reversed.

claims.text: Writing contains an indispensable cultural dimension consisting of a knowledge of the reader's knowledge—a range of knowledge tacitly shared.
claims.source_passage: ible cultural dimension of writing-a knowledge of the reader's knowledge-a range of knowledge tacitly shared.

claims.text: Experiments suggesting developmental deficits in literacy are often defective because they use culturally homogeneous subject populations.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, one of the defects in these elegant experiments was the apparent cultural homogeneity of the subject populations.

claims.text: Improvements in literate education would affect performance in experimental communication tasks.
claims.source_passage: improvements in literate education would affect performance in this kind of experimental task, which is so analogous to a writing task.

claims.text: Culturally literate adults know how to talk to strangers because they can sense what the stranger probably does and does not know.
claims.source_passage: Culturally literate adults know how to talk to strangers. Knowing what the stranger probably does and does not know, they sense when they must be prolix and when they can be brief.

claims.text: The amount of information that must be made explicit in a piece of writing is inversely proportional to the amount of information that is already shared between writer and reader.
claims.source_passage: In fact we could state this principle as a universal law for all writers: The amount of information that must be made explicit in a piece of writing is inversely proportional to the amount of information that is already shared between writer and reader.

claims.text: Prolixity and brevity in communication are purely cultural matters determined by the degree of shared cultural knowledge.
claims.source_passage: And our prolixity or brevity will depend upon our degree of shared cultural knowledge.

claims.text: A basic writer's lack of familiarity with the knowledge and expectations of his readers is a problem that faces all writers to some degree.
claims.source_passage: A basic writer's lack of familiarity with the knowledge and expectations of his readers is to some degree a problem that faces all writers.

claims.text: Most writing aims at a particular group of readers and assumes in them a particular range of common knowledge.
claims.source_passage: most writing aims at a particular group of readers, and assumes in them a particular range of common knowledge.

claims.text: Cultural literacy is identical to the shared culture that defines the common reader.
claims.source_passage: The shared culture of the common reader is what one means by cultural literacy.

claims.text: The term 'common reader' dates back to the 18th century and was popularized by Samuel Johnson.
claims.source_passage: The term "common reader" goes back to the 18th century, to Dr. Johnson.

claims.text: In the 18th century, a commonality of literate people shared a specific grammar school education and range of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: undoubtedly in Johnson's own day there did exist a commonality of literate people who shared much the same grammar school education, who had read many of the same ancient and modern authors, who continued to read many of the same periodicals... and who could be counted on to have a certain range of shared knowledge and attitudes.

claims.text: A group of approximately two hundred literate adults provides a statistical approximation of the common reader of a culture.
claims.source_passage: We also assumed that if we gathered about two hundred literate adults together in a room, we might get a statistical approximation of the common reader of our own culture and might therefore get highly duplicatable results.

claims.text: The effectiveness of writing can be measured by comparing the reading speed and comprehension of an original text versus an expert rewrite.
claims.source_passage: Our early results were highly promising, because we quickly demonstrated that a rewrite by an expert did indeed communicate more effectively than the original freshman piece, even when the length, meaning, and tone of the rewrite stayed as close as possible to the original.

claims.text: Expert stylistic revisions allow a text to be read and understood much more efficiently than a standard student essay.
claims.source_passage: The expert version was, as you see, read and understood much more efficiently than was the original paper.

claims.text: There are cultural dimensions to reading effectiveness that exist independently of style and rhetoric.
claims.source_passage: But what we had also developed, as we went on to discover, was an interesting measurement of some of the cultural dimensions that lie invisibly beyond style and rhetoric per se.

claims.text: Standard aspects of paper-grading can be empirically related to real-world reading effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: We reasoned that if some consistent difference could be measured between the effectiveness of a well-written text and a poorly written one that conveyed the same meanings, then perhaps some aspects of paper-grading could be related to the real world, with attendant benefits to students, teachers, and researchers.

claims.text: Stylistic changes recommended by composition teachers significantly improve the efficiency and understanding with which a text is read.
claims.source_passage: The expert version was, as you see, read and understood much more efficiently than was the original paper.

claims.text: The measurement of reading rates can serve as a sensitive indicator of the difference between good and bad writing quality.
claims.source_passage: we had apparently developed a rather sensitive measure of the difference between good and bad writing.

claims.text: Reading comprehension involves cultural dimensions that lie invisibly beyond style and rhetoric per se.
claims.source_passage: But what we had also developed, as we went on to discover, was an interesting measurement of some of the cultural dimensions that lie invisibly beyond style and rhetoric per se.

claims.text: Changing the order of clauses or words within a sentence to move the main idea from the beginning or end to the middle interferes with textual coherence.
claims.source_passage: What we mainly did to degrade the essays was to change the order of clauses or words within the sentences so that the main idea was put in the middle instead of at the beginning or end where the original writer had put it. This also had the effect of interfering with the coherence of the original, by separating words that linked one sentence to another.

claims.text: Topic familiarity is a decisive factor in whether stylistic quality affects reading speed.
claims.source_passage: The only difference was the familiarity of the topic for our readers.

claims.text: When a topic is unfamiliar, the cognitive effort required to process the subject matter makes the quality of the prose style irrelevant to reading speed.
claims.source_passage: What in fact happened, however, was that the topic itself required so much time and effort from the reader that the added effort induced by a poor style became irrelevant.

claims.text: Unfamiliar topics require readers to explicitly work out underlying assumptions to make the linguistic surface meaningful.
claims.source_passage: Because the topic was unfamiliar, the assumptions behind the topic (even though vaguely known to the common reader) had to be worked out explicitly in the reader's mind to make the linguistic surface meaningful.

claims.text: The perception that a subject is intrinsically difficult or abstract is often an error; the difficulty usually stems from the reader's lack of familiarity.
claims.source_passage: One's first instinct is to say that the Hegel subject was intrinsically more difficult and abstract than the Roman subject, but that is probably quite wrong. The Hegel topic was harder for these readers simply because it was less familiar to them.

claims.text: Differences in reading performance between university students and community college students on historical topics are driven by differing levels of topical familiarity.
claims.source_passage: For our community-college subjects, reading about Grant and Lee was rather like reading about Hegel’s logic in the earlier case.

claims.text: There is a finite limit to the time and effort people are willing to expend in puzzling out the meaning of a text.
claims.source_passage: This explanation assumes that there’s a limit to the time and effort people are willing to expend in puzzling out the meaning of a text.

claims.text: The negative effects of poor writing and difficult subjects likely increase as texts become longer and reader tolerance declines.
claims.source_passage: When texts are longer, reader tolerance may decline, and the effects of bad writing and hard subjects may be greater with these longer texts. This conjecture must, of course, be validated.

claims.text: When readers lack extra-linguistic knowledge of a subject, the quality of the linguistic surface becomes irrelevant to their reading process.
claims.source_passage: Community college students could do it, and could answer questions accurately, but they had to dredge up consciously so much unfamiliar, extra-linguistic material that the quality of the linguistic surface became irrelevant to them.

claims.text: The quality of writing style only makes a significant difference in reading speed and comprehension when readers are culturally literate.
claims.source_passage: From this last example, it seems warranted to suppose that the quality of writing style only begins to make a significant difference when readers are culturally literate-when they have sufficient extra-linguistic knowledge to permit an easy competence in the linguistic sphere.

claims.text: The understanding of the linguistic surface of speech or text depends upon extra-linguistic knowledge of the subject matter.
claims.source_passage: This is a pungent description of how an understanding of the linguistic surface of speech depends upon an extra-linguistic knowledge of the subject matter which the linguistic surface treats.

claims.text: Standard readability indexes like the Dale-Chall index function reliably on average because they track word familiarity and sentence length, but they fail on specific complex texts.
claims.source_passage: Such indexes to readability work reliably on the average because short, familiar words are generally used in familiar ways, and short sentences are easy to process.

claims.text: A text can possess a fourth-grade level vocabulary and syntax yet remain unreadable to most adults due to its conceptual subject matter.
claims.source_passage: Yet the linguistic surface is normal in syntax and fourth-grade level in vocabulary. Psychologically speaking, one might say that the topic is strange to those who lack a well formed "schema" for metaphysical speculation

claims.text: Readers lacking a schema for a specific topic require significantly more time to accommodate new information into their existing mental structures.
claims.source_passage: In Piagetian terms, they have difficulty in accommodating what is being said to schemata that they already possess. Thus an ordinary reader will need a great deal of time to work out ways of accommodating such a linguistic surface to more familiar schemata.

claims.text: There is an unbroken continuum from cultural literacy to reading literacy and then to writing competence.
claims.source_passage: It suggests that there exists an unbroken continuum from cultural literacy, to literacy in reading, and thence to competence in writing.

claims.text: A person cannot write better than he or she can read because the writing process requires reading and evaluating one's own stylistic choices.
claims.source_passage: How could a person possibly write better than he or she can read? One has to read one's own writing, after all, in making the most elemental stylistic choices.

claims.text: Advancement in cultural literacy is a firm pre-requisite for advancement in the skill of writing.
claims.source_passage: One is led to the conclusion that advancement in cultural literacy is a firm pre-requisite for advancement in the skill of writing.

claims.text: Training in the skills of composition, the writing process, and the 'basics' cannot convey the cultural information necessary for general literacy.
claims.source_passage: No amount of training in the skills of composition, in the writing process, and in the basics will by themselves convey the additional cultural information that underlies advancement in general literacy.

claims.text: Students cannot write effectively about a specific topic without first undergoing extensive reading to learn the subject and understand audience expectations.
claims.source_passage: Surely they could do so only after long reading, in which they not only learned about Grant and Lee, but also learned what their own readers could be expected to know about Grant and Lee.

claims.text: There is no quick fix for students' writing shortcomings through skills-based training alone.
claims.source_passage: This implies, of course, that there can be no quick fix to our students' shortcomings in writing.

claims.text: Except for spelling and motor skills, all aspects of writing (grammar, vocabulary, speech patterns) are determined by a student's cultural background.
claims.source_passage: Except for spelling, and the motor skills used in forming letters, all aspects of grammar, vocabulary, and habitual speech patterns are determined by the earlier cultural backgrounds of our students.

claims.text: A single optimal technique for writing instruction is unlikely to exist in diverse classrooms due to varying cultural starting points.
claims.source_passage: In our diverse culture, every classroom is full of students with very different cultural starting points, and this makes it highly unlikely that we will find a single optimal technique of instruction in writing skills-unless it be the method of individual tutorial instruction.

claims.text: Effective acculturation in writing must take the student's non-standard cultural starting point into account.
claims.source_passage: Because the cultural starting points of these Black children were non-standard, the techniques of acculturation should take that fact into account.

claims.text: Teachers often find the shared experiences of colleagues more useful for classroom instruction than standardized composition research.
claims.source_passage: Most of us teachers in actual classrooms have learned that the most useful composition research has been the experience of our colleagues who teach the same sorts of students as we, in the same sort of cultural setting.

claims.text: The findings of psycholinguistics and related fields are elementary and universal, and often primitive compared to the cultural complexities of actual discourse.
claims.source_passage: But their contributions are elementary and universal ones. Any teacher of Basic Writing is already dealing with cultural complexities of discourse that make the most sophisticated psycholinguistic experiments seem primitive.

claims.text: Modern psycholinguistic findings regarding writing were already well-represented in traditional 18th-century textbooks.
claims.source_passage: I had to concede that every direct application of new findings from psycholinguistics was already well-represented in traditional textbooks, some of them going back to Hugh Blair's in the eighteenth century.

claims.text: The imparting of essential information (the cultural approach) has been neglected as an integral part of writing instruction.
claims.source_passage: My point is, rather, that the cultural approach, the imparting of essential information has been neglected as an integral part of our teaching of writing.

claims.text: Writing competence can decline even when the efficiency of teaching writing as a pure skill has improved.
claims.source_passage: In short, the burden of my song is that writing competence is a deeply complex and far-reaching cultural acquisition, which has declined even where efficiency in teaching the skill of writing has advanced.

claims.text: The fragmentation of literacy instruction accelerated around 1950 when college English teachers divided into separate organizations for literature and composition.
claims.source_passage: I know from my own experience that this fragmentation has been accelerating at the college level since around 1950, when college English teachers divided themselves into two separate professional organizations teachers of literature and teachers of composition-represented by the Modern Language Association on the one hand, and the Conference on College Composition and Communication on the other.

claims.text: Every teacher of writing should ideally also be a teacher of literature and cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: Every teacher of writing should ideally be also a teacher of literature in its broadest sense. The teacher of literacy needs also to be a teacher of cultural literacy.

claims.text: The teaching of literacy in America has become unfortunately fragmented.
claims.source_passage: My first inference concerns the unfortunate fragmentation of our teaching of literacy.

claims.text: The current trend toward separate courses conducted by technocratic specialists in reading, writing, and literature is highly undesirable.
claims.source_passage: The worst of all worlds would be to have separate courses conducted by different technocratic specialists in reading, in writing, and in literature that world towards which we are now moving.

claims.text: The movement to return to 'basics' in education must be supplemented by a return to shared content and classics.
claims.source_passage: Another inference is that Back-to-the-Basics needs to be supplemented with Back-to-the-Classics: Back to Content, Shared Knowledge, Cultural Literacy.

claims.text: The belief that fragmented knowledge will naturally integrate itself in a student's mind is losing its appeal.
claims.source_passage: The hope that an invisible hand will somehow integrate the fragmented knowledge that we convey in our schools is beginning to lose its appeal, as we infer from the reinstitution of required courses in the colleges.

claims.text: There is a direct connection between the decline in writing skills and a lack of basic civic and cultural knowledge among students.
claims.source_passage: It is no accident that a report of declines in writing skills was accompanied by a report that forty-seven per cent of our seventeen-year olds-students on the verge of being voters- do not know that each state elects two senators, have no notion of the fifth amendment, believe that the President appoints members of Congress.

claims.text: Educators use the term 'pluralistic' as a code word to evade the responsibility of assessing students' actual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In this context the word "pluralistic" begins to sound like a code word for evasion of responsibility.

claims.text: Television is not the primary cause of the decline in writing competence and can actually support cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: The decline is not altogether owing to TV, which in some respects is a force for cultural literacy.

claims.text: The decline in writing competence is primarily seen in the conventions and nuances of writing rather than in basic grammar and spelling.
claims.source_passage: But in most cases the decline is in the conventions and nuances of writing, more than in grammar and spelling.

claims.text: The primary cause of writing decline is the loss of a sense of membership in a literate community, leaving students without a concept of a 'common reader.'
claims.source_passage: It seems connected with a decline in the amount of reading and writing students have done, but most of all, with the loss of a sense of membership in a literate community that provides an appropriate audience to which writing can be addressed.

claims.text: Cultural illiteracy causes writing anxiety by making writing feel like a law-breaking activity where the rules are unknown.
claims.source_passage: This new cultural illiteracy makes writing a strange and Kafkaesque activity for people who cannot possibly have a sense of a common reader to whom their writing could be directed.

claims.text: Writing anxiety is caused by a student's uncertainty regarding how their writing will be understood and valued by an audience.
claims.source_passage: Holland found that the chief cause of their fear and disorientation is their uncertainty about how their writing will be responded to-not just how it will be graded, but how it will be understood and valued.

claims.text: A totally regimented curriculum is unfeasible and will not be accepted in a country as diverse and independent-minded as the United States.
claims.source_passage: But, beyond this agreed-upon, narrow core of knowledge, a totally regimented curriculum is quite unfeasible. The knowledge most needful changes over the years, and, in any case, educational regimentation is not going to be accepted in this diverse and independent-minded country.

claims.text: Cultural literacy can be achieved by teaching shared 'types' of knowledge (e.g., any one Shakespeare tragedy) rather than strictly identical texts.
claims.source_passage: So, for us, the idea of cultural literacy must entail not just shared pieces of knowledge, but also shared types of knowledge. Even if all high school graduates do not read Hamlet, perhaps all of them could read one tragedy by Shakespeare?

claims.text: Every citizen should know a narrow core of basic facts about their government, such as whether a U.S. senator is elected or appointed.
claims.source_passage: I have no doubt that there are some things we want every citizen to know-for instance, whether a U.S. senator is elected by the people or appointed by the President, and so on.

claims.text: Cultural literacy entails shared types of knowledge (schemata) in addition to specific shared pieces of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: So, for us, the idea of cultural literacy must entail not just shared pieces of knowledge, but also shared types of knowledge.

claims.text: Schemata are structural requirements for both reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: Psycholinguists have shown that these typical structures, called "schemata", are required for both reading and writing.

claims.text: Shared schemata can be constructed from similar rather than identical materials.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, these schemata can be shared even when they are built up from similar rather than identical materials. Reading one nineteenth century novel is about as useful as reading another in building up these complex convention systems.

claims.text: The teaching of cultural literacy requires explicit agreement on both the kinds of materials and specific facts to be taught.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, the teaching of cultural literacy cannot be haphazard. It will not take care of itself. It requires us to agree about the kinds of materials we shall teach, and also about some of the particular facts and texts we shall teach.

claims.text: Establishing a central shared education is the most pressing subject for the advancement of literacy.
claims.source_passage: It would certainly be useful to literacy if this idea of a central shared education were at least being discussed more widely than is now being done. I believe that no subject is more pressing for the advancement of literacy.

claims.text: Composition teachers should be trained in subject matters like literature rather than being isolated specialists.
claims.source_passage: It has renewed my sense of the rightness of training composition teachers in subject matters, including literature, and the wrongness of sustaining a separate class of composition specialists who teach nothing else, and who come to think of writing as a craft and a subject in its own right.

claims.text: Writing is a craft that belongs to a wider literate culture which teachers must exemplify.
claims.source_passage: On the contrary, writing is a craft that is part of a much wider literate culture which the teacher should not only teach but also exemplify.

claims.text: All social classes use both restricted and elaborated linguistic codes.
claims.source_passage: Like many others, I decline to accept Bernstein's correlation of restricted code with lower class speech. All social classes use both kinds of codes.

claims.text: Adopting the posture of an 'Arnoldian sage' would have been an inauthentic and inappropriate stance for the author's address on literacy.
claims.source_passage: But Mina also had a powerful streak of common sense and she would certainly have advised me against trying to adopt in this talk the inauthentic posture of an Arnoldian sage.

claims.text: Specialists in the craft of composition are currently in high demand for teaching positions and research funding for the field is abundant.
claims.source_passage: specialists in the craft of composition are in great demand for teaching posts. Money for composition research is easy to come by.

claims.text: Large-scale financial investments in writing instruction, such as Yale's million-dollar grant, will improve the craft of writing primarily because they refocus attention on the problem.
claims.source_passage: And all of this effort is certainly going to improve instruction in the craft of writing, if only because we are again paying attention to the problem instead of neglecting it.

claims.text: The author is strongly in favor of research into the writing process and does not believe the skills-based approach to writing has been overstressed, only that the cultural approach has been neglected.
claims.source_passage: I am strongly in favor of this research. ... I am not trying to suggest that the skills approach to writing has been overstressed. Anyone who writes knows that writing skills cannot possibly be overstressed or overtaught. My point is, rather, that the cultural approach... has been neglected.

claims.text: The success of the Black parents in the Ann Arbor court case was based on the common-sense principle that teachers must understand students' non-standard cultural starting points to acculturate them effectively.
claims.source_passage: Because the cultural starting points of these Black children were non-standard, the techniques of acculturation should take that fact into account. That was the common sense behind the ideological rhetoric in the case; and that is surely why the Black parents won.

claims.text: The teaching of cultural literacy cannot be haphazard or left to spontaneous integration; it requires explicit agreement on the materials, facts, and texts to be taught.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, the teaching of cultural literacy cannot be haphazard. It will not take care of itself. It requires us to agree about the kinds of materials we shall teach, and also about some of the particular facts and texts we shall teach.

claims.text: Cultural commonality can be established through 'shared types' of knowledge, meaning that reading any one Shakespeare tragedy or watching any episode of a show like MASH provides the necessary schemata.
claims.source_passage: The cultural commonality would then partly reside in shared types of experience, and common types of knowledge is what cultural literacy partly means. ... Reading one nineteenth century novel is about as useful as reading another in building up these complex convention systems. Seeing one episode of MASH will be as useful as seeing another.

claims.text: Writing becomes a 'Kafkaesque' activity for students when they lack a membership in a literate community that provides a dependable and understandable readership.
claims.source_passage: This new cultural illiteracy makes writing a strange and Kafkaesque activity for people who cannot possibly have a sense of a common reader to whom their writing could be directed.

claims.text: The author previously held sanguine expectations for the direct application of psycholinguistics to composition before concluding that such insights are often already present in traditional textbooks.
claims.source_passage: Here again, I speak as a newly-won convert away from my earlier sanguine expectations. ... I had to concede that every direct application of new findings from psycholinguistics was already well-represented in traditional textbooks.

claims.text: Difficulties in reading texts like Plato's Parmenides arise because the reader lacks a 'schema' for metaphysical speculation rather than because of linguistic complexity.
claims.source_passage: Psychologically speaking, one might say that the topic is strange to those who lack a well formed 'schema' for metaphysical speculation, because they have not been exposed to other passages like this one.

evidence.description: Yale University's allocation of a $1.25 million grant to improve undergraduate writing, which Hirsch cites as an example of the current massive investment in the craft-approach.
Experimental results from 1972 regarding how individuals perceive and remember sentence entailments rather than just linguistic traces.
evidence.source_passage: In 1972 there were published some experimental results which clinched the point I am making-even if on a very primitive level-yet making it all the more persuasively, since the experimental tests were so obvious and elemental.

evidence.description: Large-scale experiments at the University of Virginia sponsored by the National Endowment for the Humanities.
evidence.source_reference: National Endowment for the Humanities experiments at Virginia

evidence.description: A 1972 experiment showing that subjects remember the inferred meaning of a sentence (e.g., a fish swimming beneath both turtles and a log) as being explicitly stated, even when it was only implied by one sentence version.
Experiments by Kraus and Glucksberg where subjects must describe unfamiliar shapes to a partner behind a barrier, mimicking the conditions of writing.
Data from Kraus and Glucksberg showing that while literate adults score 100% on shape-ordering tasks, fifteen-year-olds take seven to nine trials to achieve perfect results.
Comparison of developmental stages showing that children from nursery school through seventh grade consistently fail to complete error-free trials in the shape-ordering task.

An experiment by a Harvard undergraduate in Cambridge, MA, where a questioner asking for directions received brief answers when appearing to be a native and long, detailed (prolix) answers when signaling he was an 'out-of-towner'.
Kraus and Glucksberg's finding that adults use more elaborated (prolix) descriptions than children when tasked with describing strange shapes to a listener.
University of Virginia composition experiment using 200 readers, digital clocks, and a comparison of freshman essays versus expert rewrites.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch et al., University of Virginia (c. 1980s)

evidence.description: Quintile graphs comparing reading rates for control essays and student/expert essays, showing significant efficiency gains for the expert versions.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch et al., University of Virginia research graphs

evidence.description: The historical shared curriculum of 18th-century grammar schools.
evidence.source_reference: General historical reference to Dr. Johnson's era

evidence.description: An experiment comparing a B-minus student essay with an expert revision. The expert version, containing only stylistic changes, was read and understood much more efficiently.

An experiment using passages from Durant’s 'History of Civilization'. For the familiar topic (Roman baths), good style increased reading speed. For the unfamiliar topic (Hegel's logic), style had no effect on speed as readers were 50 wpm slower across both versions.

An experiment using Bruce Catton’s text on Grant and Lee with university and community college students. Community college students' performance mirrored the university students' performance on Hegel, indicating a lack of familiarity with the subject matter.

A quintile graph showing the reading rates of community college students for Catton's 'Grant and Lee' in both original and stylistically degraded versions, demonstrating no significant speed benefit from the original's better style.

A comparison of quintile graphs showing that while university audiences read stylistically improved versions of a 'Friendship' essay faster, community college audiences showed the same pattern for simple topics, illustrating the role of familiarity.
An analysis by Robert Gordon showing that a passage from Plato's Parmenides tests at a fourth-grade level on the Dale-Chall index but is cognitively 'unreadable' without metaphysical knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Robert Gordon, 'The Readability of an Unreadable Text,' English Journal

evidence.description: An anecdote about a Princeton matron who understood Einstein's words but could not understand the lecture because she lacked the extra-linguistic subject matter knowledge.
A court case in Ann Arbor regarding the need for teachers to understand the speech conventions of Black students.
The author's five-year immersion in psycholinguistic research while writing a book on composition theory.
Comparison of modern psycholinguistic findings with 18th-century rhetoric textbooks by Hugh Blair.
The 1950 split of college English teachers into the MLA and the CCCC.
The reinstitution of required core courses in colleges as evidence of the failure of elective-heavy systems.
A report showing 47% of 17-year-olds lack basic civic knowledge (e.g., number of senators, 5th Amendment).
The author's personal review of 3,000 freshman essays from universities and community colleges.
Mina Shaughnessy's book 'Errors and Expectations' detailing the anxieties of basic writers.
Professor Morris Holland's psychological observations of acute anxiety symptoms in basic writers at UCLA.
Psycholinguistic research demonstrating that schemata (structural knowledge) are necessary for processing text.
Example of how watching different episodes of the television show MASH builds similar cognitive frameworks.
The work of Mina Shaughnessy as an example of integrating the teaching of literature with the teaching of basic writing.
Study on sentence memory showing the constructive nature of cognitive processing.
evidence.source_reference: J.D. Bransford, J.R. Barclay, and J.J. Franks (1972)

evidence.description: Research on social and nonsocial speech patterns.
evidence.source_reference: R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg (1977)

concepts.author_definition: An orientation toward literacy that focuses on the visible 'process and product' of writing, including syntax, organization, rhetoric, spelling, and coherence.
concepts.common_alternative: Often treated as the entirety of writing instruction.
concepts.source_passage: That is why virtually all that is written about composition devotes itself to the craft of writing-to coherence, to pre-writing, organization, syntax, sentence variety, and the like.

concepts.author_definition: A whole system of unspoken knowledge shared between writer and reader, including expectations about form, the world, and what others know.
concepts.source_passage: In short, the cultural dimension is that whole system of unspoken, tacit knowledge that is shared between writer and reader.

concepts.author_definition: A style or persona characterized by social purpose, integrity, eloquence, and a specific sense of style, modeled after Matthew Arnold.
concepts.source_passage: It should be a subject, I thought, that has an Arnoldian ring to it, which is why I chose the title "Culture and Literacy" with its allusion to Arnold's great book Culture and Anarchy.

concepts.author_definition: An invisible, culturally shared component that linguistic model builders use to explain how readers understand extra-linguistic implications in text.
A deficiency in the shared background knowledge required to anticipate a reader's expectations and effectively communicate in writing.
Knowledge of what others know; the shared culture of the 'common reader' that allows for efficient communication.
An imagined audience for whom a writer posits a particular range of shared common knowledge and attitudes, allowing the writer to determine what must be made explicit.
concepts.common_alternative: Historically associated with Samuel Johnson's 18th-century commonality of literate people.

concepts.author_definition: A sociolinguistic distinction (from Bernstein) between prolix/detailed communication (elaborated) and concise/shorthand communication (restricted) based on the relationship and shared knowledge between speakers.
A methodological tool where a computer divides readers into five groups based on reading rates to visually compare how different groups deal with different texts.

A methodology for testing reading efficiency by systematically changing the order of clauses or words within sentences to move the main idea away from the beginning or end without changing the vocabulary.
The invisible background knowledge and cultural assumptions that exist beyond rhetoric and determine a reader's ability to process a text's 'linguistic surface'.
The common but (according to Hirsch) often mistaken belief that certain topics like philosophy are naturally harder to process than descriptive history, regardless of reader background.
Measures of text difficulty based on formal linguistic traits like word frequency (familiarity), average sentence length, and syllable counts.
concepts.common_alternative: Often treated as objective measures of a child's reading level (Lexiles).
concepts.source_passage: As you know, these readability scores are based on the familiarity of words, the average sentence length, and the number of syllables in a 100 word passage.

concepts.author_definition: Knowledge of the subject matter that exists outside the grammar and vocabulary of the language itself, necessary for interpreting the meaning of words.
Mental structures used to organize and interpret information; Hirsch specifically uses the Piagetian concept of accommodation to explain reading.
concepts.source_passage: Psychologically speaking, one might say that the topic is strange to those who lack a well formed "schema" for metaphysical speculation... they have difficulty in accommodating what is being said to schemata that they already possess.

concepts.author_definition: The non-standard or varied cultural backgrounds and speech conventions that students bring into the classroom, which determine their mastery of grammar and vocabulary.
Research based on the practical, shared experiences of classroom teachers working in specific cultural settings rather than abstract or standardized scientific experiments.
A complex cultural acquisition that goes beyond motor skills and spelling to include the ability to navigate cultural complexities and audience expectations.
A disabling psychological state, comparable to math anxiety, caused by a lack of membership in a literate community and uncertainty about audience interpretation.
A rhetorical shield used by educators to avoid the responsibility of defining and assessing a common core of knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: In this context the word "pluralistic" begins to sound like a code word for evasion of responsibility.

concepts.author_definition: The dual responsibility of the English teacher to teach both literature (subject matter) and literacy (writing/reading skills) as an integrated whole.
A figure representing literate culture at its best, characterized by social purpose, integrity, eloquence, and a sense of style, often integrating roles as poet, essayist, and critic.
A state of writing disorientation where the writer knows they are being judged by rules or laws (literary conventions) they do not understand and has no sense of a common reader.
Specialists who treat reading, writing, and literature as fragmented, technical skills rather than integrated dimensions of a wider literate culture.
The explicit, literal words and syntax of a sentence, contrasted with the invisible inferences and cultural entailments that readers actually remember.

cases.name: The Turtle Experiment (1972)
cases.description: Subjects were presented with sentences about turtles on a log and a fish swimming. Results showed they could not distinguish between a sentence that explicitly stated the fish was under the turtles and one that only implied it, proving that mental models of the world supersede literal linguistic memory.
key_passages: The tests concerned whether we perceive and remember what a sentence says as a linguistic trace, or whether we perceive and remember, instead, what the linguistic traces invisibly entail.

For the subjects, the inference that the fish were underneath both the turtles and the log was actually stated by the sentence and was remembered as being explicitly, linguistically stated even though it was not.

cases.name: Kraus and Glucksberg Communication Task
cases.description: A study involving subjects separated by a barrier who must communicate how to order abstract, unfamiliar shapes. It compared the performance of adults against children of various ages (nursery to 15-year-olds).
key_passages: The communicative task was to explain to the other subject how to order a series of unfamiliar shapes.

What fascinates me is the mediocre performance of the fifteen-year-olds. The American fifteen-year-old subjects chosen by Kraus and Glucksberg were the products of eight or nine years in our school systems.

cases.name: The Harvard Street Experiment
cases.description: A researcher approached passersby in Cambridge, Massachusetts, asking for directions to Central Square. In the first phase, he acted as a native (Boston accent, local newspaper) and received a five-word answer ('First Stop on the Subway'). In the second phase, he adopted a rural Missouri accent to signal he was 'from out of town' and received a highly detailed, redundant set of instructions.
key_passages: the researcher goes out on the streets of Cambridge, Massachusetts, with a hidden tape recorder, and approaches passersby with the following question: 'How d'ya get to Central Square?'
Invariably, he gets a very brief reply to his question... For this answer to be adequate, consider just some of the knowledge which the dozens of passersby had to assume that the questioner also knew
In this second phase of the experiment... this was the typical sort of result: 'Yes. You go downstairs into the Subway... and take the train headed for Quincy, but you get off very soon...'

cases.name: University of Virginia Composition Research
cases.description: A study conducted by Hirsch and colleagues involving approximately 200 literate adults. Participants read booklets containing either an original freshman essay or an expert rewrite. Their reading times were measured using a large digital clock, and their comprehension was tested via questionnaires. The study used quintile analysis to compare reading efficiency, discovering that expert stylistic changes significantly improved reading speed and comprehension efficiency.
key_passages: In a typical presentation we gathered about two hundred people in a lecture room, in the front of which was a big digital clock that kept time in seconds.
Our early results were highly promising, because we quickly demonstrated that a rewrite by an expert did indeed communicate more effectively than the original freshman piece

The left-hand graph shows how the two groups performed when they read the same essay, and it shows quite convincingly that our shuffling of the booklets had paired off two very similar groups of readers.

cases.name: Durant's 'Epicurean Rome' vs 'Hegel'
cases.description: A controlled experiment using texts from William and Ariel Durant. Readers were tested on their reading rates for original and stylistically degraded versions of a text on Roman baths and a text on Hegel's logic. Results showed that style only mattered for the familiar Roman topic; the Hegel topic was so unfamiliar that the stylistic quality became irrelevant to the reading rate.
key_passages: The essay on the left was a descriptive piece on everyday life in Ancient Rome... The essay on the right was an explanation of Hegel's conception of logic as metaphysics.
What in fact happened, however, was that the topic itself required so much time and effort from the reader that the added effort induced by a poor style became irrelevant.

cases.name: Catton's 'Grant and Lee' across audiences
cases.description: A study comparing university students and community college students reading Bruce Catton's history. The community college students showed the same 'Hegel effect' (style irrelevance) on the Grant and Lee text that university students showed on Hegel, demonstrating that the 'difficulty' was relative to the audience's background knowledge.
key_passages: In this case our original text was a passage from Bruce Catton that contrasted the personalities of Ulysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee.
For our community-college subjects, reading about Grant and Lee was rather like reading about Hegel’s logic in the earlier case.

cases.name: The Friendship Essay Experiment
cases.description: University and community college audiences were given a simple student essay on the topic of friendship. Both groups showed faster reading rates when provided with an expert revision (stylistic improvement). This contrasts with the 'Grant and Lee' case, where the community college group did not benefit from better style because the topic was unfamiliar.
key_passages: the university audiences and the community college audiences are reading a simple student essay on friendship-along with its expert revision.
From this last example, it seems warranted to suppose that the quality of writing style only begins to make a significant difference when readers are culturally literate

cases.name: Ann Arbor Court Case
cases.description: A publicized court case in which parents of Black children successfully argued that teachers should learn the children's speech conventions to effectively teach them standard conventions. The author views this as a common-sense victory for recognizing cultural starting points in education.
key_passages: This insight was the real point at issue in the recent, much-publicized court case in Ann Arbor where parents of Black children argued successfully that white teachers should learn the speech conventions of Black children in order to lead them effectively into the standard conventions.

cases.name: The Professional Split of 1950
cases.description: The division of college English departments into two separate organizations: the Modern Language Association (MLA) for literature and the Conference on College Composition and Communication (CCCC) for composition.
key_passages: I know from my own experience that this fragmentation has been accelerating at the college level since around 1950, when college English teachers divided themselves into two separate professional organizations teachers of literature and teachers of composition

cases.name: US College English Split (1950)
cases.description: The institutional division of English departments into the MLA (literature) and CCCC (composition), which the author cites as the beginning of the fragmentation of literacy instruction.
key_passages: this fragmentation has been accelerating at the college level since around 1950, when college English teachers divided themselves into two separate professional organizations teachers of literature and teachers of composition

cases.name: United States Curriculum
cases.description: The author argues that while a narrow core of shared knowledge is necessary for citizenship, the diverse and independent nature of the US makes a 'totally regimented' national curriculum unfeasible.
key_passages: educational regimentation is not going to be accepted in this diverse and independent-minded country.

thinkers.name: Mina Shaughnessy
thinkers.key_idea: The importance of teaching 'basic writers' and focusing on research rather than political maneuvering in the composition field.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch views her as a mentor figure whose work on basic writing provides the foundation for his shift toward investigating the cultural dimensions of literacy.
key_passages: Go back to Virginia and get your grants, do your research, and write your books.
I will focus on her main interest, the teaching of writing, and I will bring to bear some of our recent research at Virginia.

thinkers.name: Matthew Arnold
thinkers.key_idea: The relationship between culture and social order (Culture and Anarchy).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch alludes to Arnold's work to frame the high stakes of literacy and its cultural mission.
key_passages: I chose the title 'Culture and Literacy' with its allusion to Arnold's great book Culture and Anarchy.

thinkers.name: Kraus and Glucksberg
thinkers.key_idea: Communication effectiveness can be measured by the ability to describe unfamiliar objects to another person using only words.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their data to show that American students lack the shared knowledge necessary for effective writing, though he critiques their study's lack of demographic and literacy-level diversity.
key_passages: This extra-linguistic dimension was approached from another angle in some experiments reported by Kraus and Glucksberg.
Indeed, one of the defects in these elegant experiments was the apparent cultural homogeneity of the subject populations.

thinkers.name: Basil Bernstein
thinkers.key_idea: The sociolinguistic distinction between 'elaborated' and 'restricted' codes.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bernstein's codes to explain why adults are 'prolix' when talking to strangers (elaborated code) and brief when talking to those with whom they share knowledge (restricted code).
key_passages: this fact is highly reminiscent of Bernstein's sociolinguistic distinction between elaborated and restricted codes-which is a technical version of the distinction between prolixity and conciseness.

thinkers.name: Samuel Johnson
thinkers.key_idea: The 'common reader' whose common sense and shared knowledge base define literacy and poetical standards.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch traces the concept of the 'common reader' back to Johnson to show that literacy has always depended on a community sharing a specific range of knowledge and attitudes.
key_passages: The term 'common reader' goes back to the 18th century, to Dr. Johnson. 'I rejoice to concur with the common reader,' he remarked in his Life of Gray

thinkers.name: William and Ariel Durant
thinkers.key_idea: Authors of 'History of Civilization', used as a source of stylistically consistent but topically diverse material for reading experiments.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their work as a control for style in his experiments on topic familiarity.
key_passages: The texts we used were passages from the multi-volumed History of Civilization by William and Ariel Durant... written in a similar style, and all directed to the same readers.

thinkers.name: Bruce Catton
thinkers.key_idea: Author of historical texts (specifically Grant and Lee) used to test the background knowledge of different student populations.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch utilizes Catton's prose to demonstrate that even 'accessible' history becomes difficult for students lacking specific historical background knowledge.
key_passages: our original text was a passage from Bruce Catton that contrasted the personalities of Ulysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee.

thinkers.name: Robert Gordon
thinkers.key_idea: Readability metrics can be deceptive if they ignore the conceptual 'schema' required to understand a text.
thinkers.source_work: The Readability of an Unreadable Text
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Gordon's analysis of Plato to prove that simple vocabulary does not equal simple comprehension.
key_passages: just recently, I ran across a very clever and specific example of this phenomenon in the most recent issue of the English Journal. It was called 'The Readability of an Unreadable Text,' by Robert Gordon.

thinkers.name: Jean Piaget
thinkers.key_idea: Knowledge acquisition involves 'accommodation' of new information into existing 'schemata.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch applies Piaget's cognitive terms to the act of reading and the necessity of background knowledge.
key_passages: In Piagetian terms, they have difficulty in accommodating what is being said to schemata that they already possess.

thinkers.name: Hugh Blair
thinkers.key_idea: Author of traditional 18th-century textbooks on rhetoric and writing.
thinkers.engagement: The author notes that Blair's 18th-century work already contained the useful applications that modern psycholinguistics claims to have newly discovered.
key_passages: I had to concede that every direct application of new findings from psycholinguistics was already well-represented in traditional textbooks, some of them going back to Hugh Blair's in the eighteenth century.

thinkers.name: Morris Holland
thinkers.key_idea: The chief cause of writing anxiety is uncertainty about how the writing will be understood and valued by an audience.
thinkers.engagement: The author uses Holland's psychological interviews to link writing failure to the absence of a 'literate community'.
key_passages: Professor Morris Holland of UCLA, a psychologist, has observed in basic writers the classical symptoms... Holland found that the chief cause of their fear and disorientation is their uncertainty about how their writing will be responded to

thinkers.name: Christopher Lasch
thinkers.key_idea: The 'new illiteracy' within a narcissistic culture.
thinkers.engagement: Cited in the further reading section as a source on the current state of illiteracy.
key_passages: Christopher Lasch, (1978), The Culture of Narcissism... Chapter 5, 'The New Illiteracy.'

actors.name: Mina Shaughnessy
actors.context: Director of the CUNY basic writing program and author of Errors and Expectations.

actors.name: Yale University
actors.context: Cited as an example of institutions spending large sums on 'craft' approaches to writing instruction.

actors.name: National Endowment for the Humanities
actors.context: Sponsor of Hirsch's research at the University of Virginia.

actors.name: Psycholinguists
actors.context: Researchers who have recently begun studying the 'invisible' dimension of speech.

actors.name: American fifteen-year-olds
actors.context: The subject group that showed mediocre performance in communication tasks despite years of schooling.

actors.name: Kraus and Glucksberg
actors.context: Researchers whose experiments on communicative competence are critiqued by Hirsch for ignoring cultural homogeneity.

actors.name: Harvard undergraduate
actors.context: The researcher who conducted the Cambridge street experiment.

actors.name: University of Virginia
actors.context: Site where the composition research and reading rate experiments were conducted.

actors.name: Samuel Johnson
actors.context: 18th-century writer whose 'common reader' concept provides the foundation for Hirsch's theory.

actors.name: University Students
actors.context: The audience group that found the Roman baths familiar but Hegel unfamiliar.

actors.name: Community College Students
actors.context: The audience group that found Bruce Catton's 'Grant and Lee' as unfamiliar as university students found Hegel.

actors.name: Community College Students
actors.context: Subjects in Hirsch's reading speed experiments who struggled with unfamiliar topics regardless of writing quality.

actors.name: Princeton matron
actors.context: The subject of an anecdote used to illustrate the difference between understanding words and understanding subject matter.

actors.name: Ann Arbor Parents
actors.context: Successful litigants who argued for the recognition of Black children's speech conventions.

actors.name: Modern Language Association (MLA)
actors.context: Professional organization for literature teachers.

actors.name: Conference on College Composition and Communication (CCCC)
actors.context: Professional organization for composition teachers.

actors.name: Modern Language Association (MLA)
actors.context: Cited as one half of the professional split that fragmented literacy teaching.

actors.name: Conference on College Composition and Communication (CCCC)
actors.context: Cited as the other half of the professional split that fragmented literacy teaching.

actors.name: Chronicle of Higher Education
actors.context: Source of the report that educators are attacking knowledge assessment as inappropriate for a pluralistic society.

actors.name: Psycholinguists
actors.context: Cited for proving that schemata are required for reading and writing.

actors.name: Composition Specialists
actors.context: A 'separate class' of teachers who view writing as an isolated craft rather than part of a wider culture.

objections_raised.objection: The poor performance of young children in communication tasks is due to developmental stages (egocentricity).
objections_raised.response: While this may explain young children, it does not explain the mediocre performance of 15-year-olds, who are not trapped in egocentricity but simply lack shared cultural knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: The text on Hegel is intrinsically more difficult and abstract than a text on Roman baths.
objections_raised.response: This is incorrect; if the experiment were conducted with philosophers, the difficulty levels would likely equalize. The difficulty is a function of familiarity, not the inherent nature of the topic.

objections_raised.objection: Improving the 'basics' and the 'writing process' is the best way to help students who struggle with writing.
objections_raised.response: No amount of skills training will compensate for the lack of cultural information that actually underlies literacy and writing competence.

objections_raised.objection: The author is suggesting that writing skills should not be taught or that the skills approach has been overstressed.
objections_raised.response: Writing skills are essential and cannot be overtaught; however, the 'cultural approach' (the necessity of information) has been neglected in the process.

objections_raised.objection: Does a focus on cultural literacy lead logically to a 'Napoleonic' educational system where everyone is taught exactly the same thing?
objections_raised.response: Not entirely. While a narrow core of shared facts/texts is necessary, a totally regimented curriculum is unfeasible in the US. Instead, we can aim for shared 'types' of knowledge (e.g., any Shakespeare play) to maintain diversity while ensuring commonality.

objections_raised.objection: A push for shared knowledge implies an educational system where everyone is taught exactly the same things, which sounds like regimentation.
objections_raised.response: A totally regimented curriculum is unfeasible, but we must still agree on a narrow core of facts and 'types' of knowledge (schemata) to ensure literacy.

dependencies.explanation: The dominance of literature on the craft of writing provides evidence of the neglect of the cultural dimension.
The claim that craft is only half the story depends on the existence of a huge domain of tacit knowledge that the craft approach ignores.
C10 defines the specific components (topic knowledge and reader expectations) that make up the invisible base of the iceberg metaphor.
Empirical evidence regarding how memory prioritizes entailments over linguistic traces supports the idea of a shared unspoken system.
If literacy is a shared system of unspoken knowledge, it follows that the writer must possess a sense of the reader's specific knowledge range.
By ruling out mechanics (vocabulary/grammar) as the cause of failure, the author clears the path for education (cultural knowledge) as the solution.
The claim that education is the antidote relies on the premise that there are no biological or developmental barriers to 15-year-olds being literate.
The ability of literate adults to sense a stranger's knowledge (C24) provides the mechanism for the 'indispensable cultural dimension' of writing (C21).
The principle of inverse proportionality (C25) relies on the existence of a 'tacitly shared' dimension of knowledge (C21) to act as the divisor.
If prolixity and brevity are determined by shared knowledge (C26), then it follows that explicit information is inversely proportional to that shared knowledge (C25).
If most writing assumes a particular range of common knowledge (C28), then 'cultural literacy' can be defined as the possession of that specific shared culture (C29).
The 18th-century grammar school education (C31) serves as a historical example of the 'shared culture' that defines the common reader (C29).
While expert stylistic revisions improve efficiency (C34), the author argues this improvement is bound by cultural dimensions that exist independently of that style (C35).
C39 restates the claim of C35, emphasizing that these dimensions are 'invisible' and lie 'beyond' the mechanics of style.
The claim that style is irrelevant for unfamiliar topics rests on the premise that the cognitive burden of reconstructing meaning from scratch consumes all available mental resources.
If style doesn't matter for unfamiliar topics but does for familiar ones, then topic familiarity is the deciding factor in style's impact.
The conclusion that cultural literacy is required for style to matter depends on the principle that without extra-linguistic knowledge, the linguistic surface (style) is processed inefficiently.
The theoretical dependence of text understanding on extra-linguistic knowledge provides the mechanism for why style only matters to the culturally literate.
The necessity of reading and evaluating one's own work creates a logical link between reading ability and writing competence.
Advancement in writing can only be a prerequisite if there is an unbroken continuum connecting it to cultural literacy.
If cultural literacy is a prerequisite for writing, then skills-only training (which lacks content) is inherently insufficient for general literacy.
Empirical evidence that students need to read extensively to write effectively reinforces the idea that skills training alone is a 'quick fix' that fails.
The example of a 4th-grade level text being unreadable due to subject matter demonstrates why standard readability indexes fail on specific complex texts.
The link between writing skills and cultural knowledge (C74) is explained by the specific mechanism of students losing the concept of a 'common reader' (C78).
If cultural illiteracy causes writing anxiety by making the rules feel unknown, it supports the idea that the writer has lost their sense of membership in a stable literate community.
The claim that the cultural approach has been neglected (C66) relies on the empirical observation that literacy instruction has become fragmented and separated (C70).
The normative rejection of technocratic specialists in reading/writing (C71) leads to the conclusion that teachers must integrate literature and cultural literacy into writing instruction (C69).
The theoretical connection between knowledge and skill (C74) provides the rationale for why neglecting the cultural approach (C66) is a failure of instruction.
The claim that cultural literacy requires shared schemata depends on the theoretical premise that schemata are structural requirements for reading and writing.
If schemata can be constructed from similar rather than identical materials, then cultural literacy can be defined by shared types of knowledge rather than just specific facts.
The idea of teaching Shakespearean tragedies as a 'type' is a practical application of the theoretical concept that cultural literacy involves shared schemata.
The necessity for explicit agreement on materials is driven by the reality that a totally regimented, identical curriculum is unfeasible in a diverse nation.
If writing becomes 'Kafkaesque' (meaningless/absurd) without a literate community, establishing a shared education is the primary solution for restoring literacy.
To move from shared types (theoretical) to actual teaching (normative), there must be an explicit agreement on what those materials and facts are.
The Ann Arbor case shows that teachers must understand cultural starting points to acculturate students, which supports the need for an explicit, non-haphazard approach to teaching literacy.

C101 provides a specific instance (Plato's Parmenides) to support the broader claim that 'readability levels' based on linguistic complexity are scientifically incorrect compared to schema-based models.
The claim that a lack of schema causes reading difficulty depends on the premise that verbal comprehension is contingent upon unstated background knowledge.
If the difficulty is conceptual/schematic rather than linguistic, it reinforces the idea that reading is not a general 'skill' but is domain-specific.

warrants.warrant_text: If elite institutions like Yale dedicate massive resources exclusively to writing-as-craft, it indicates a systemic educational bias that ignores non-craft variables.
warrants.vulnerability: The grant might actually include content-based initiatives not detailed in the author's summary.

warrants.warrant_text: If human memory treats implied information as if it were explicitly stated, then the meaning of a text is constructed from shared inferences rather than just the words on the page.
warrants.vulnerability: Inferences about spatial relations (turtles/logs) might be universal cognitive processes rather than 'cultural' knowledge.

warrants.warrant_text: If 'good style' fails to improve reading speed when the topic is unfamiliar (Hegel), then linguistic craft is secondary to prior knowledge in determining literacy.
warrants.vulnerability: Hegel may be uniquely difficult due to conceptual complexity rather than just a lack of shared cultural facts.

warrants.warrant_text: Language users automatically adjust the volume of explicit information based on their perception of the recipient's background knowledge, proving that tacit knowledge dictates the visible output.
warrants.vulnerability: Social code-switching in directions is a specialized speech act that may not perfectly map to the cognitive demands of formal writing.

warrants.warrant_text: If communication failure in experiments correlates with cultural homogeneity or lack of shared context rather than age, then the limiting factor is cultural knowledge rather than innate cognitive development.
warrants.vulnerability: The author assumes that 15-year-olds fail due to schooling deficits rather than the possibility that 'knowledge of the reader's knowledge' is a late-developing cognitive social skill (theory of mind) regardless of curriculum.

warrants.warrant_text: Communication is a zero-sum game of information transfer where data is either explicitly stated in the signal or implicitly retrieved from the receiver's memory.
warrants.vulnerability: Redundancy is often a feature of effective communication even when knowledge is shared; 'brevity' is not always the goal of literate adults.

warrants.warrant_text: If even the best stylistic improvements (rhetoric) cannot overcome a reader's lack of background knowledge, then comprehension must depend on something outside of the text's surface features.
warrants.vulnerability: The author may be underestimating the power of 'plain language' and clear style to help readers bridge knowledge gaps through context clues.

warrants.warrant_text: Differences in performance on the same task across different demographics (University vs. College) can be attributed to the variable of background knowledge if the cognitive tasks are otherwise identical.
warrants.vulnerability: Assumes no other variables (like IQ or prior general reading volume) differ significantly between the two groups.

warrants.warrant_text: If simple language (4th-grade level) does not guarantee comprehension when content is unfamiliar, then the 'difficulty' is located in the concepts/familiarity, not the linguistic structure.
warrants.vulnerability: A critic might argue that 'abstract' concepts are inherently harder to form schemata for, regardless of vocabulary.

warrants.warrant_text: If effective writing on a specific topic requires first learning the subject matter, then general writing ability cannot be separated from domain-specific or cultural knowledge.
warrants.vulnerability: Does not account for the possibility of 'transferable' rhetorical structures that once learned can be applied to new content.

warrants.warrant_text: If reading processing speed (a measure of literacy) is dictated more by topic familiarity than by stylistic clarity, then the ability to produce coherent text (writing skill) must also be fundamentally constrained by the author's grasp of the knowledge they assume their audience shares.
warrants.vulnerability: Assumes a perfect symmetry between the cognitive loads of decoding (reading) and encoding (writing).

warrants.warrant_text: A text can be linguistically 'simple' by all objective metrics (vocabulary, syntax) yet be intellectually impenetrable without specific cultural/philosophical schemas, proving that 'skill-based' readability metrics ignore the primary driver of comprehension.
warrants.vulnerability: Critics might argue that Plato's text is unique in its abstraction and not representative of the functional literacy needed for everyday citizenship.

warrants.warrant_text: Since basic mechanics (grammar/spelling) are intact while social nuances are failing, the root cause must be social/contextual (loss of community) rather than technical (loss of instruction).
warrants.vulnerability: The decline in nuances could simply be a result of less formal instruction in rhetoric and style rather than a lack of 'community membership'.

warrants.warrant_text: Structural equivalence: If two different cultural artifacts (e.g., MASH and a play) activate the same underlying mental framework, they are functionally identical for the purpose of building literacy schemata.
warrants.vulnerability: Literary critics would argue that the 'schema' of a sitcom and a Shakespearean tragedy are not equivalent in linguistic complexity or cultural weight.

warrants.warrant_text: Social communication principle: Writing is not an isolated mechanical skill but a social act that requires a predictable 'other' who shares one's context.
warrants.vulnerability: Critics of expressivist writing might argue that writing can be a tool for self-discovery independent of a shared readership.

warrants.warrant_text: The bridge principle: To move a student from a non-standard culture to a standard literate culture, the curriculum must explicitly map the path rather than assuming it will happen naturally.
warrants.vulnerability: One could argue this justifies 'multiculturalism' rather than 'acculturation' into a single national literate culture.

warrants.warrant_text: If high-level texts are difficult because of missing conceptual frameworks (schemata) rather than sentence length or word choice, then formulas measuring only the latter (readability) are fundamentally flawed.
